title: Coffee and walnut cake
subtitle: The British classic, properly coffee-flavoured
excerpt: A two-layer coffee sponge sandwiched and finished with a coffee buttercream, decorated with walnut halves. The coffee must be strong enough to flavour the crumb; a teaspoon of instant in the batter produces nothing.
sourceNotes: Coffee and walnut cake appears in British household baking guides from the late Victorian period. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents walnut cake; the coffee version became a staple after affordable ground and instant coffee became widespread in the early 20th century. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records the combination. The recipe structure here follows the creaming method that has been standard since Acton.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers individually, well wrapped. Defrost at room temperature and frost on the day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge layers keep in an airtight tin for 2 days before frosting. Once assembled, the cake keeps for 2 days at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Both tins on the same shelf; swap halfway on a conventional oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: A concentrated liquid coffee flavouring, far stronger than brewed coffee and used in small amounts to flavour cake batter and buttercream without adding significant extra liquid. Camp Coffee (a chicory-and-coffee liquid) or a proprietary coffee paste both work. If using espresso, use only 1–2 tablespoons and reduce another liquid in the recipe accordingly.

content.text: The coffee here must be strong or the cake is just a walnut sponge with a beige frosting.
or a very concentrated shot of espresso is what actually delivers the flavour. The walnuts go in the batter and onto the top; they are load-bearing, not decorative.

attrs.prepNote: or 2 tbsp very strong cooled espresso

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases. Beat the butter and sugar together until pale and fluffy; this takes 5 minutes with a hand mixer. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour and baking powder, then the coffee extract and chopped walnuts. Divide evenly between the tins and smooth the surfaces. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the sponge springs back to a light press and a skewer comes out clean. Leave in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool.

Beat the butter until very soft. Add the icing sugar in two batches and beat until pale and smooth, about 3 minutes. Beat in the coffee extract. If the buttercream is stiff, add milk a teaspoon at a time to bring it to a spreading consistency. Place one cooled sponge on a plate and spread with half the buttercream. Place the second sponge on top and spread the remainder over the top; leave the sides plain for the classic version. Press walnut halves around the edge or arrange them in a ring on top.
